narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your LSAT score of 36 and undergraduate GPA of 3.0, while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 62.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam—above the typical threshold for approval, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction stemmed primarily from your academic rankings in school, where your Year 1 decile of 2 and Year 3 decile of 3 placed you in the lower third of your peers, well below the average deciles of around 5-6 for most students. These early and later grade positions were the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as they signal challenges in keeping pace academically compared to others who succeeded on the bar. Your LSAT score, though solid and close to the dataset average of 36.7, provided the biggest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward success and showing your potential in standardized testing. Meanwhile, your upper-middle income bracket had a moderate influence tilting toward higher risk, possibly reflecting patterns in the data where such backgrounds didn't always correlate with bar passage strength. Your undergraduate GPA, slightly below the average of 3.21, added a small push toward failure, while your full-time enrollment plan had minimal impact in a positive direction, as it's the norm for over 90% of students. Overall, your profile stands out as having weaker school deciles than typical admitted students, who often rank higher and pass the bar more reliably. While this is disappointing, it's not a definitive verdict on your abilities—many overcome similar starts through targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking courses or boosting your GPA in advanced studies to improve your decile rankings, and perhaps seek bar prep resources early; reapplying after strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor. We're rooting for your success in law.
